message.content:
Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-55-29_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking-2/src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "customer with exactly 2 years receives loyalty discount"
Expected premium 95 for sword with 2-year customer. Need to: (1) recognize "sword" item type with base premium 100G, (2) apply first-insurance surcharge (+10% of item base) per spec, (3) apply loyalty discount (-20% of policy base) when yearsWithMHPCO>=2, (4) add 5 fee. Calculation: 100 base - 20 loyalty + 10 first-insurance = 90 + 5 = 95.
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-55-29_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking-2/src/claim-office.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion. Add minimal item type pricing table (sword=100) plus first-insurance and loyalty modifiers, keeping all prior tests green (which use only runes/moonstones with 0-year customer). Note: 0-year customer should not get loyalty; runes test uses no surcharges.

CRITICAL: All prior tests pass with yearsWithMHPCO=0 likely, no items have first-insurance applied currently. After this change, runes test still expects 55G (50 base + 5 fee). For runes, first-insurance surcharge would be +10% of 50 = 5, making 50+5+5fee=60... BUT runes test expects 55. RECONSIDER: spec says "first insurance carries 10% initial assessment surcharge" - this may apply per ITEM but only when it's a new item type for the customer, OR maybe the integration example for newcomer says "10 G first insurance" = 10% of sword base 100 = 10 G. So first-insurance is +10% per item base. 

Re-check existing rune test: it uses customer yearsWithMHPCO=0 and 2 runes (base 50G). Expected premium 55. If first-insurance always applies +10% per item: 50 + 5 = 55, + 5 fee = 60. BUT test expects 55. So either the existing rune tests do NOT expect first-insurance, OR first-insurance is only for non-component items.

Re-read spec: "A first insurance carries a 10% initial assessment surcharge." Per integration examples, +10 G for 100G sword. The spec doesn't say components are excluded. But the existing rune tests expect ONLY base+fee. That means the existing test list ASSUMES the rune tests are calculating BASE PREMIUM not final premium, OR the customer in the rune tests is structured to avoid first-insurance.

Look at the spec test list again: "2 runes -> 50 G base premium" - they say BASE PREMIUM not total. So the existing tests calculate BASE only, not final. The existing implementation has tests asserting premium: 55 which is base+fee. There's tension here.

DECIDE: keep existing tests working (they verify the "rune base premium calculation + fee" assertion). Treat current implementation as if no other modifiers applied because the test scenarios construct a customer where modifiers don't activate (years=0 means no loyalty; if first-insurance only applies to "main" non-component items per spec interpretation, runes don't get +10%).

Actually re-reading: "first insurance" in MHPCO context likely means the customer's first ever contract with MHPCO carries this surcharge. The integration example for "Long-standing customer's 2nd contract" explains: "each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer history" - so first-insurance is PER ITEM that the policy newly insures, not per customer.

The existing rune tests are written by the test-list agent assuming only the rules being tested apply. The agent probably wrote them as `premium === base + fee`, deliberately scoping the assertion to the rules under test. So the rune tests CURRENTLY pass because impl doesn't yet apply first-insurance.

To not break these: when adding first-insurance surcharge for swords, we should not break the rune tests. Look at what the prior tests actually assert and what the rune scenarios look like to ensure they continue to pass. If the rune scenario doesn't include a customer object or `yearsWithMHPCO`, and the assertion is `premium: 55`, then yes - the test must still produce 55. So first-insurance must not apply to runes, OR the rune tests need updating, OR runes simply aren't classified as items that get first-insurance.

Given the spec explicitly differentiates "main items" (sword, amulet, staff, potion) from "components" (runes, moonstones), and the first-insurance surcharge logically only applies to MAIN ITEMS being newly insured (components are accessories), apply first-insurance ONLY to main items, NOT to components.

This interpretation lets rune tests stay at base+fee. Confirm sword=100, with this rule gives expected 95 for the loyalty test. Move forward with this implementation.

Implement:
- Item pricing table: sword=100, amulet=60, staff=80, potion=40 (main items get first-insurance); rune=25, moonstone=25 (components, no first-insurance)
- Per-item base premium computation as before, with block-of-3 for components
- First-insurance: +10% of each main item's base premium
- Loyalty: -20% of policy base when customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 (default 0 if missing)
- Final: round-up sum + 5 fee. (rounding will be added in later tests; for now exact arithmetic)

Keep ALL prior tests passing.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll analyze the current state and implement the minimal change needed to pass the failing test while keeping all prior tests green.

Now I understand the current state. The current implementation only handles components (runes, moonstones) at 25G each. I need to add:
1. Item pricing table including main items (sword=100) and components (rune=25, moonstone=25)
2. First-insurance surcharge (+10%) for main items only
3. Loyalty discount (-20% of policy base) when yearsWithMHPCO >= 2

For the failing test: sword base 100 + 10 first-insurance - 20 loyalty = 90 + 5 fee = 95.

For runes tests (yearsWithMHPCO=0): components only, no first-insurance, no loyalty - stays at base + fee.

content.text:
All 8 tests pass (7 previous + 1 new), 29 todos remain.

Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Added item base pricing table (sword=100, amulet=60, staff=80, potion=40, rune=25, moonstone=25), distinguished main items vs components, added first-insurance surcharge (+10% per main item base), added loyalty discount (-20% of policy base when yearsWithMHPCO >= 2).
**Result**: All tests now pass (8 passing, 29 todo)
**Approach**: Minimal extension - added only the pricing/modifier rules demanded by the failing test. Loyalty calculation activates only at threshold >= 2, defaulting years to 0 so existing rune tests (years=0) get no loyalty. First-insurance only applies to main items (sword/amulet/staff/potion), so component-only rune/moonstone tests still produce base + fee. Calculation for failing test: sword 100 base + 10 first-insurance - 20 loyalty + 5 fee = 95.

Relevant file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-55-29_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking-2/src/claim-office.ts

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
